terms.definition: The top 100 orchestrators by total bonded stake eligible to receive work each round.
A system that perceives its environment and acts autonomously to achieve goals, often powered by large language models with tool access.
The simulation of human intelligence processes by machines — including learning, reasoning, and problem-solving — using statistical models trained on large datasets.
A Layer-2 Optimistic Rollup settling to Ethereum that processes transactions off-chain while inheriting Ethereum-grade security.
The number of bits conveyed or processed per unit of time; in video it determines the data per second of content, affecting quality and file size.
Unix timestamps embedded in each Ethereum or Arbitrum block header, used by smart contracts for time-dependent functions such as round boundaries.
The core Livepeer smart contract managing bonding, delegation, staking logic, and fund ownership on Arbitrum.
A Livepeer smart contract tracking stake-weighted voting power snapshots for on-chain governance polls on Arbitrum L2.
The mechanism connecting two blockchain ecosystems to enable token or information transfer between them.
The legacy Livepeer term for a node that published streams and submitted video for transcoding; now replaced by the term 'Gateway.'
A deployment pattern allowing teams to supply custom Docker containers running Python workloads for Livepeer AI streaming pipelines.
An AI service or job type — defined as a pipeline and model pair — that an orchestrator advertises as available to gateways.
A geographically distributed network of servers caching and delivering content with high availability and low latency to end users.
The primary general-purpose processor in a computer; in Livepeer, CPU handles node software overhead while GPU handles intensive transcoding and AI inference workloads.
A text-based interface for interacting with software by typing commands, used to configure and operate Livepeer gateway and orchestrator nodes.
Software or hardware that compresses and decompresses digital video using a defined algorithm, typically employing lossy compression.
The latency incurred when an AI model must be loaded from storage into GPU memory before it can serve its first inference request, typically 5–90 seconds.
A Livepeer project implementing a ComfyUI custom node that runs real-time media workflows for AI-powered video and audio processing on live streams.
CLI flags and environment variables that control node behavior including payment settings, pricing, preferred orchestrators, and network mode.
A neural network architecture that adds spatial conditioning controls — such as edge maps, depth, or pose — to pretrained diffusion models.
The Livepeer registry smart contract managing all protocol contract addresses and coordinating protocol upgrades on Arbitrum.
An organization governed by smart contracts in which rules are encoded in code and members vote on proposals via a decentralized ledger.
An adaptive bitrate streaming protocol that breaks content into small HTTP-served segments at multiple bitrate levels, allowing clients to switch quality dynamically.
A marketplace of GPU resources contributed by orchestrators worldwide, coordinated through crypto-economic incentives for video transcoding and AI inference.
The act of LPT holders staking their tokens toward an orchestrator they trust, sharing in rewards without needing to run infrastructure themselves.
A token holder who stakes LPT to an orchestrator to help secure the network, participate in governance, and earn a share of rewards.
The stage in the video pipeline where encoded content is transmitted from origin servers or CDNs to end-user devices for playback.
A category of blockchain systems that incentivize communities to collectively build and operate physical or computational infrastructure using token rewards.
Anyone building applications using Livepeer, typically through hosted services such as Livepeer Studio, Daydream, or Streamplace, or via library SDKs.
The abstraction layer providing tools, APIs, dashboards, and hosted services — including Livepeer Studio, Daydream, and Streamplace — for building applications on top of Livepeer.
A class of generative neural networks that learn to produce data by iteratively reversing a gradual noising process applied during training.
The native cryptocurrency of Ethereum, used to pay transaction fees (gas) and as the settlement currency for Livepeer transcoding and AI inference fees.
A 42-character hexadecimal string beginning with '0x,' derived from the last 20 bytes of a public key hash, used to send and receive funds and interact with smart contracts.
The percentage of ETH job fees that an orchestrator retains before distributing the remainder to their delegators.
The pool of accumulated ETH fees earned from transcoding and AI inference jobs, distributed between orchestrators and their delegators based on stake weight and commission settings.
The unit measuring computational effort required to execute operations on Ethereum or Arbitrum; users pay gas fees in ETH to cover execution costs.
A Livepeer node that submits jobs, routes work to orchestrators, manages payment flows, and provides a direct interface to the protocol.
The on-chain system of rules and processes for protocol decision-making, including LIP proposals, stake-weighted voting, and treasury allocation.
The Livepeer on-chain governance contract that authorizes protocol upgrades and parameter changes via token-weighted delegated voting.
A specialized parallel processor originally designed for rendering graphics, now the primary hardware for video transcoding and AI inference.
An adaptive bitrate streaming protocol developed by Apple that encodes video into multiple quality levels and serves them as segmented playlists over HTTP.
An AI platform and open-source community providing model repositories, datasets, and inference APIs; a primary source for AI models deployed on Livepeer orchestrator nodes.
A fundraising mechanism where a blockchain project sells newly created tokens to the public in exchange for established cryptocurrencies.
The dynamic issuance of new LPT each round, distributed to orchestrators and delegators based on their participation, designed to incentivize staking toward a 50% target bonding rate.
Running a trained machine learning model on new input data to produce predictions, classifications, or generated outputs.
The process of receiving a live video stream from a broadcaster's encoder into a media server, typically via RTMP, SRT, or WebRTC.
A high-level interpreted scripting language used for web and server-side development; Livepeer's primary SDKs and gateway clients expose JavaScript/TypeScript APIs.
The delay between video capture at the source and display on the viewer's device, accumulating at every stage of the pipeline.
A system characteristic where the delay between an event occurring and a response being delivered is minimised; in Livepeer, sub-500ms round-trip times are targeted for real-time AI video pipelines.
A separate blockchain built on top of a Layer-1 base chain (such as Ethereum) that handles transactions off-chain while inheriting the security guarantees of the base layer.
A participant in the Livepeer protocol or network — human or machine — that performs a defined role such as submitting jobs, providing compute, verifying work, or securing the system.
The full set of projects, tools, participants, and organizations building on or contributing to the Livepeer network, including SPEs, community tools, integrated applications, and partner services.
The official protocol explorer web interface for viewing network state, orchestrator information, staking data, and governance proposals.
A non-profit Cayman Islands Foundation Company stewarding the long-term vision, ecosystem growth, and core development of the Livepeer network.
The live, operational decentralized system of orchestrators, workers, gateways, and broadcasters performing video transcoding and AI inference jobs.
The on-chain ruleset and smart contract logic governing staking, delegation, inflation, orchestrator selection, slashing, and probabilistic payments.
An open-source media server library providing live video functionality including RTMP ingest and HLS output, used as the foundation for Livepeer transcoding nodes.
The ERC-20 governance and staking token used for orchestrator selection, delegation, reward distribution, and protocol security.
Livepeer's algorithm for decentralized token distribution at genesis using Merkle proofs to fairly distribute initial LPT supply.
The Livepeer smart contract responsible for minting new LPT tokens during reward calls and holding ETH collected from winning payment tickets.
A mathematical structure — a neural network with learned weights — enabling predictions or content generation for new inputs.
The phenomenon where a network's value increases as more participants join, creating compounding benefits for all existing members.
Any computing device running Livepeer software and participating in protocol operations — gateway nodes, orchestrator nodes, or worker nodes.
Activities, computations, or data exchanges that occur outside the main blockchain and are not directly recorded on-chain.
Actions, computations, or data that are directly recorded, executed, and verified on the blockchain with full transparency.
A protocol-governed smart contract pool of LPT funded by a portion of each round's inflation, allocated to ecosystem development through community-approved proposals.
Software whose source code is freely available for anyone to view, use, modify, and redistribute.
A supply-side node operator contributing GPU resources to the Livepeer network, receiving jobs from gateways, performing video transcoding or AI inference, and earning LPT rewards plus ETH fees.
A signed off-chain data structure issued by a gateway to an orchestrator representing a probabilistic payment, with a configured chance of being redeemable for ETH on-chain.
A configured end-to-end AI processing workflow that defines the data flow from input through one or more model inference steps to produce output.
The on-chain governance and incentive layer that coordinates orchestrators, staking rewards, inflation, slashing, and job payments via smart contracts on Arbitrum.
A main participant performing a core function in the Livepeer protocol: gateways, orchestrators, and delegators.
An ordered set of encoding profiles from lowest to highest quality used for adaptive bitrate rendition selection in video delivery.
Running machine learning models on live streaming input with latency low enough for interactive speeds, typically under 100 milliseconds.
A service that holds private keys securely in an isolated environment and signs transactions on behalf of a Livepeer gateway or orchestrator node.
A single encoded version of a source video at a specific resolution, bitrate, and codec configuration.
A measure of an orchestrator's performance, reliability, and trustworthiness that influences job routing priority and payment selection by gateways.
The percentage of inflationary LPT rewards that an orchestrator retains before distributing the remainder to their delegators.
Layer-2 scaling solutions that execute transactions off-chain and post compressed data or proofs to Layer 1 to inherit its security guarantees.
A discrete time interval measured in Ethereum/Arbitrum blocks during which staking rewards are calculated and protocol state is updated.
A TCP-based protocol for streaming audio, video, and data over the internet, historically operating on port 1935, widely used for live video ingest.
An advanced open-source text-to-image diffusion model with a larger UNet and dual text encoders producing high-resolution 1024×1024 images.
A short time-sliced chunk of multiplexed audio and video that is independently transcoded, enabling parallel processing across multiple orchestrators.
A penalty mechanism that destroys a portion of an orchestrator's bonded LPT for defined protocol violations such as failing verification or submitting fraudulent results.
The network-defined rules specifying exactly when and how LPT is slashed: failing verification checks, skipping assigned verifications, or sustained underperformance.
A treasury-funded team or organization with a defined scope, budget, and accountability structure, funded by the Livepeer on-chain treasury to deliver specific protocol or ecosystem objectives.
LPT locked in the Livepeer protocol via bonding, representing a participant's commitment and determining their share of rewards and work allocation.
A model where staking the protocol's native token is required to perform work or access network services, converting participation into token buying pressure.
Locking tokens in a proof-of-stake protocol to participate in network security, governance, and earn rewards.
Continuous delivery of audio and video content over a network, rendered in real time as it is received rather than requiring a full download before playback.
The total potential revenue opportunity available to a product or service if it captured 100% of its target market.
NVIDIA's inference SDK that optimizes trained models through quantization, layer fusion, and kernel tuning to deliver low-latency GPU inference.
The economic design of a token system encompassing supply, distribution, incentive structures, staking mechanics, inflation curves, and governance rights.
Direct digital-to-digital conversion of video from one encoding to another — changing format, resolution, bitrate, or codec — to produce multiple adaptive renditions.
A neural network architecture that uses self-attention mechanisms to process sequential data in parallel, forming the basis of most modern large language models and many vision models.
The pool of LPT and ETH held on-chain for funding public goods, ecosystem development, and community-approved initiatives.
A network component responsible for validating work performed by orchestrators, confirming that transcoded or AI-processed output matches expected results.
An AI model already loaded into GPU memory and ready to serve inference requests immediately without cold-start delay.
An open-source project and W3C standard providing browsers and mobile apps with real-time peer-to-peer audio, video, and data exchange without plugins.
A protocol enabling viewers to receive WebRTC streams from servers via SDP offer/answer exchange over standard HTTP, used for browser-based sub-second latency playback.
A media delivery model where recorded video content is stored server-side and streamed to viewers on request at any time, in contrast to live streaming.
A Livepeer node running Docker containers for AI models or transcoding processes, executing compute tasks delegated by an orchestrator.
A neural network that represents and predicts environment dynamics, enabling an agent to plan future actions by simulating outcomes without interacting with the real environment.
